content.full_text:
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 ••• 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814 
Administration 
(916) 322·5662 
322·5660 
John Kramer, Administrator 
Bear Valley Community Hospital 
P.O. Box 1732 
Big Bear Lake, CA 
92315 
Dear Mr. Kramer: 
Executive/Legal 
322·5901 
April 14, 1981 
Enforcement 
322-6441 
'~OL{~ /D7 
Statements of Economic Interest 
322-6444 
This is in response to your request for advice on the 
conflicts of interest provisions of the Political Reform 
Act. 
Please forgive our delay in responding. 
The facts, as I understand them, are as follows: 
Bear Valley Community Hospital District is a local 
hospital district whose powers are vested in a five member 
board of directors elected by the District's voters. 
The 
District owns and operates the Bear Valley Community Hospital, 
a general acute care hospital. 
A physician, Dane Winkelman, has recently been elected 
to the District's Board of Directors. 
Dr. Winkelman has a 
private medical practice in Big Bear Lake limited primarily 
to Obstetrics, Gynecology and Infertility. 
His office is 
located in a medical building adjacent to the hospital. 
This building is owned by a partnership in which Dr. Winkelman 
has an 11% interest. 
Five of the remaining eight partners 
are also physicians who are on the medical staff. 
Dr. Winkelman shares office space with the Big Bear 
Lake Medical Group, a California corporation. 
Pursuant to 
an oral agreement, the Group provides Dr. Winkelman with 
building services, office space, and pays for certain supplies 
and other expenses in exchange for a percentage of his pro-
fessional fee. 
Dr. Winkelman owns no stock in Big Bear Lake 
Medical Group and has no relationship with it other than 
that described above. 
The Fair Political Practices Commission is charged with 
administration and enforcement of the Political Reform Act. 
Unlike other state or local provisions, such as Government 


John Kramer 
April 14, 1981 
Page Two 
Code Section 1090,l/ the Act does not regulate contractual 
relationships. 
Nor does the Act focus on the issue of incompatible 
activities. 
We cannot advise you as to three of the questions 
you posed because they relate solely to these issues. 
The two remaining questions, which relate to the Political 
Reform Act, are as follows: 
1. 
Must a Board member who is also a member of 
the medical staff abstain from participation in certain 
deliberations and decisions of the Board, such as those 
related to medical staff privileges, appointments, 
recommendations and suspensions? 
2. 
Must a Board member who is also a member of 
the medical staff refrain from decisions affecting the 
medical staff privileges of physicians who are business 
partners of the physician-director? 
The conflict of interest provisions of the Political 
Reform Act are found in Sections 87100-87103, which provide 
in relevant part that 
No public official at any level of state or local 
government shall make, participate in making or in any 
way attempt to use his official position to influence a 
governmental decision in which he knows or has reason 
to know he has a financial interest. 
Section 87100. 
An official has a financial interest in a decision 
within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably 
foreseeable that the decision will have a material 
financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on 
the public generally, on: 
(a) 
Any business entity in which the public 
official has a direct or indirect investment worth 
more than one thousand dollars ($1,000). 
(b) 
Any real property in which the public 
official has a direct or indirect interest worth 
more than one thousand dollars ($1,000). 
l/ All statutory references made are to the Government 
Code unless otherwise noted. 


John Kramer 
April 14, 1981 
Page Three 
(c) 
Any source of income, other than loans 
by a commercial lending institution in the regular 
course of business on terms available to the public 
without regard to official status, aggregating two 
hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more in value 
provided to, received by or promised to the public 
official within 12 months prior to the time when 
the decision is made. 
(d) 
Any business entity in which the public 
official is a director, officer, partner, trustee, 
employee or holds any position of management. 
For purposes of this section, indirect investment or 
interest means any investment or interest owned by the spouse 
or dependent child of a public official, by an agent on 
behalf of a public official, or by a business entity or 
trust in which the official, the official's agents, spouse, 
and dependent children own directly, indirectly or beneficially 
a 10 percent interest or greater. 
Section 87103. 
Dr. Winkelman has a financial interest in his medical 
practice because it is a source of income to him of more 
than $250 a year. 
Therefore, he cannot make or participate 
in making any decision as a Board member which would foresee-
ably have a material financial effect on his medical practice. 
Although the question of disqualification always depends on 
specific circumstances, it is likely that decisions related 
to his own medical staff privileges will have a beneficial 
or detrimental effect on his practice. 
If this is the case, 
he should not use his official position to influence these 
decisions. 
He should also refrain from participating in 
decisions on staff privileges, appointments, suspensions and 
recommendations when those could have a material financial 
effect on his medical practice. 
For instance, if he is the 
only infertility specialist on the staff, he should probably 
disqualify himself from participating in a decision on appoint-
ment of another infertility specialist to the staff, if such 
a decision would have a material financial effect on his 
practice due to increased competition. 
Dr. Winkelman has a financial interest in the medical 
building partnership, within the meaning of Section 87103, 
because: 
1. 
He has an investment in the partnership worth 
more than $1,000~ 


John Kramer 
April 14, 1981 
Page Four 
2. 
He has an investment in real property worth 
more than $1,000; and 
3. 
The partnership may also be a source of income 
to him. 
Therefore, he must disqualify himself from the decision 
making process to the extent that these will be a significant 
financial effect on the partnership. 
I do not have enough 
information to predict which decisions could produce such a 
result. 
It is possible, however, that decisions affecting 
staff privileges of any of the other five physician-partners 
would materially effect the income to the partnership. 
The facts are a little confusing as to the relationship 
between Dr. Winkelman and Big Bear Medical Group. 
In document 
(g), Dr. Winkelman describes himself as an independent con-
tractor associated with the Group and says that the Group 
provides him with office space. 
On the other hand, your 
letter says that the office space is owned by the partnership 
and you characterize the relationship between the parties as 
that of a contract for services. 
Since the facts are not 
entirely clear, I cannot determine whether or not Dr. Winkelman 
has a financial interest in the Big Bear Medical Group; it 
appears that he does not. 
Assuming this to be the case, 
Dr. Winkelman could fully participate in decisions affecting 
Big Bear Medical Group. 
I hope this information has been helpful to you. 
Again, 
please forgive the delay. 
SCL:kp 
Very truly yours, 
Stella Connell Levy 
Counsel 
Legal Division 


BEAR VALLEY COMMUNITY HOSPITAL 
41870 GARSTIN 
P.o. BOX 1732 
February 3, 1981 
Ted Prim, Executive Director 
Fair Political Practices Commission 
1100 K street 
Post Office Box 807 
Sacramento, California 95804 
BIG BEAR LAKE, CA 92315 
Re: 
Conflict of Interest -- Request for Opinion 
Dear Mr. Prim; 
A PUBLIC ENTITY 
(714) 866-7526 
Bear Valley Community Hospital District is a local hospital district 
constituted and existing pursuant to Health and Safety Code Sections 
32000 and following. The powers of the District are vested in a five-
member board of directors elected by the voters of the District. The 
District owns and operates the Bear Valley Community Hospital, a general 
acute-care hospital located at Big Bear Lake, California. 
At the District election held on November 4, 1980, a physician who is 
also a member of the Hospital's Medical Staff was elected to membership 
on the District's Board of Directors. The inter-relationships of the 
Board of Directors with the Hospital's medical staff and with various 
providers of professional services prompts the District to ask for the 
opinion of the Fair Political Practices Commission as to the questions 
set forth below. 
In order to facilitate your consideration of those questions, I have enclosed 
a copy of each of the following documents: 
(a) 
By-laws of the Bear Valley Community Hospital District. 
(b) 
The By-Laws and Rules and Regulations of the Medical Staff 
of Bear Valley Community Hospital, Big Bear Lake, California. 
(c) 
Conflict of Interest and Disclosure of Personal Finances 
Code for Bear Valley Community Hospital District. 
(d) 
Various representative minutes of meetings of the District 
Board of Directors including minutes for January 15, 1980; 
February 26, 1980; April 15, 1980; June 17, 1980; July 22, 1980; 
August 19, 1980; September 16, 1980; and October 21, 1980. 
(e) 
Opinion No. 78-44 of the County Counsel of San Bernardino, 
issued on October 9, 1978. 
(f) 
A Memorandum of Agreement, dated November 20, 1979, governing 
the provision of emergency room services to the Hospital 
until December 1, 1980 by the Big Bear Lake Medical Group. 
ACCREDITED BY THE JOINT COMMISSION ON ACCREDITATION OF HOSPITALS 


Bear Valley Community Hospital, Conflict of Interest--Request for Opinion 
page 2 
(g) 
Letter of January 20, 1981. 
The Physician whose election has raised the questions set forth below has 
a 
medical practice in Big Bear Lake limited to primarily Obstetrics, 
Gynecology and Infertility. His medical office is located in a medical 
building adjacent to the Hospital. The medical building is owned by a 
partnership in which the physician-director has an 11% interest. Five 
of the remaining eight partners in the medical building are also physicians 
who are on the medical staff of the Hospital. 
The physician-director shares office space with the Big Bear Lake Medical 
Group, the group which provided emergency room services to the Hospital 
until December 1, 1980. Pursuant to an oral agreement, the group provides 
billing services and office personnel to the physician-director and ln 
return therefore receives a percentage of the physician-director's 
monthly billings. Beyond this, there is no contractual, employment, 
partnership or agency relationship between the group and the physician-
director. 
There is no contractual, employment, partnership or agency relationship 
between the physician-director and the present provider of emergency 
room services to the Hospital. However, the physician-director may in 
the future wish to work in the Hospital's emergency room as an independent 
contractor of the then current provider of emergency room services for the 
Hospital. In such a situation, payment of the physician-director's 
compensation would be made by the contractor and not by the Hospital or the 
District. 
Considering the foregoing circumstances and the attached materials, and 
especially the power of the District Board of Directors in respect to 
appointment to, reappointment to, and suspension from, the medical staff 
of the District's Hospital, the District requests that you furnish your 
opinions on the following questions: 
1. 
Is there any inherent conflict of interest between being a member of 
the District Board of Directors and a member of the Hospital medical 
staff such that one person may not faithfully discharge the duties 
of both positions? 
2. 
If no such inherent conflict of interest exists, should a person who 
holds both positions nevertheless abstain from participation in 
certain deliberations and decisions of the District Board of 
Directors, such as those related to medical staff privileges, 
appointments and suspensions or other Medical Staff recommendations 
coming before the District Board? If so, should the physician-director 
abstain only where a matter would likely affect himself financially, 
or should he abstain in all such matters? 
3. 
In the circumstances set forth above which relate to the specific 
medical staff member who was elected to the District Board of 
Directors, is there any actual or potential conflict of interest 
such that the physician-director would be disqualified from acting 
in some matters which might come before the Board of Directors 


near Valley Commun 
Hospital, Conflict of In 
est-- Request for Opinior 
page 3 
for action? For example, questions of medical staff privileges 
relating to physicians who are business partners with the physician-
director? 
4. 
Maya physician who is a member of the District Board of Directors 
accept with an independent contractor status arrangement to act 
as a duty physician in the emergency room of the District's Hospital? 
Does it make any difference that the Hospital and the District would 
have a contractual relationship for such services only with the 
third-party contractor and not with the physician-director? Does it 
make any difference if all compensation accruing to the physician-
director for such services were to be paid by the third-party 
contractor and not by the Hospital? 
5. 
Presuming the physician-director is an independent contractor 
associated with Big Bear Lake Medical Group as described in the 
accompanying document (g), may the District enter into contracts 
with Big Bear Lake Medical Group and if so, in what circumstances 
would this be acceptable? 
If you have any question, or if more information is needed, please 
telephone to me at (714) 866-7526 or to the District's attorney 
in this matter, Edward Duddy, Deputy County Counsel, at (714) 383-3811. 
I 
am sure you can appreciate that speedy resolution of the foregoing 
questions is important to the orderly conduct of the District's affairs. 
For that reason we will appreciate whatever priority you can assign 
to our problem. 
Very 
Enclosures 
xcc: 
Members of the Board of Directors 
Edward Duddy, Deputy County Counsel 
JK/gr

For that reason we will appreciate whatever priority you can assign

Very

Edward Duddy, Deputy County Counsel

sections.conclusion: Dr. Winkelman has a financial interest in his medical practice as a source of income exceeding $250 annually and therefore cannot participate in decisions that would foreseeably have a material financial effect on his practice. He should disqualify himself from decisions on his own medical staff privileges and from decisions on staff privileges of other physicians where such decisions could materially affect his practice. He must also disqualify himself from decisions materially affecting the medical building partnership in which he holds an 11% interest. The facts regarding his relationship with Big Bear Medical Group are unclear, but assuming he has no financial interest in it, he could fully participate in decisions affecting that entity.
sections.question_synthetic: Does a physician board member of a hospital district who is also on the hospital's medical staff and has financial interests in a medical building partnership and medical group have conflicts of interest requiring disqualification from certain board decisions regarding medical staff privileges and appointments?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Under the Political Reform Act, Dr. Winkelman must disqualify himself from board decisions that foreseeably have a material financial effect on his medical practice and on the medical building partnership in which he has an 11% interest. He should abstain from decisions regarding medical staff privileges that could create competitive effects on his practice or affect the partnership's income from other physician-partners.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: ocr; LLM: Document includes both incoming request (dated February 3, 1981) and FPPC response letter (dated April 14, 1981). Extracted from the response letter's Question and Conclusion sections. The document clearly identifies the two Political Reform Act questions, distinguishing them from other questions the FPPC declined to answer relating to Government Code Section 1090 and incompatible activities.

For that reason we will appreciate whatever priority you can assign

Very 
Enclosures

Edward Duddy, Deputy County Counsel

CONCLUSION: Dr. Winkelman has a financial interest in his medical practice as a source of income exceeding $250 annually and therefore cannot participate in decisions that would foreseeably have a material financial effect on his practice. He should disqualify himself from decisions on his own medical staff privileges and from decisions on staff privileges of other physicians where such decisions could materially affect his practice. He must also disqualify himself from decisions materially affecting the medical building partnership in which he holds an 11% interest. The facts regarding his relationship with Big Bear Medical Group are unclear, but assuming he has no financial interest in it, he could fully participate in decisions affecting that entity.

embedding.summary: The FPPC advises Bear Valley Community Hospital District on conflicts of interest for a physician board member who also practices medicine and has financial interests in a medical building partnership. The FPPC concludes the physician must disqualify himself from decisions materially affecting his medical practice and the partnership.
